[$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")12,500,000 (1958, entire complex) [IDR](/wiki/Indonesian_rupiah "Indonesian rupiah")769.69 billion (2016–2018, equal to about US$58,5 billion with 2016 exchange rate)[[3]](#cite_note-4)
[Friedrich Silaban](/wiki/Friedrich_Silaban "Friedrich Silaban") (entire Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex)[[2]](#cite_note-3)

([Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"): *Stadion Utama Gelora Bung Karno*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Bung Karno Sports Arena Main Stadium'; abbreviated as **SUGBK** or **GBK**), formerly **Senayan Main Stadium** and **Gelora Senayan Main Stadium**, is a [multi-purpose stadium](/wiki/Multi-purpose_stadium "Multi-purpose stadium") located at the center of the [Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Sports_Complex "Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex") in [Central Jakarta](/wiki/Central_Jakarta "Central Jakarta"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). It is mostly used for [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches, and usually used by the [Indonesia national football team](/wiki/Indonesia_national_football_team "Indonesia national football team") and [Super League](/wiki/Super_League_(Indonesia) "Super League (Indonesia)") club [Persija Jakarta](/wiki/Persija_Jakarta "Persija Jakarta"). The stadium is named after [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno"), the then-[president of Indonesia](/wiki/President_of_Indonesia "President of Indonesia"), who sparked the idea of building the sports complex.

**Gelora Bung Karno Main Stadium** ([Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"): *Stadion Utama Gelora Bung Karno*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Bung Karno Sports Arena Main Stadium'; abbreviated as **SUGBK** or **GBK**), formerly **Senayan Main Stadium** and **Gelora Senayan Main Stadium**, is a [multi-purpose stadium](/wiki/Multi-purpose_stadium "Multi-purpose stadium") located at the center of the [Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Sports_Complex "Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex") in [Central Jakarta](/wiki/Central_Jakarta "Central Jakarta"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). It is mostly used for [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches, and usually used by the [Indonesia national football team](/wiki/Indonesia_national_football_team "Indonesia national football team") and [Super League](/wiki/Super_League_(Indonesia) "Super League (Indonesia)") club [Persija Jakarta](/wiki/Persija_Jakarta "Persija Jakarta"). The stadium is named after [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno"), the then-[president of Indonesia](/wiki/President_of_Indonesia "President of Indonesia"), who sparked the idea of building the sports complex.

When first opened prior to the [1962 Asian Games](/wiki/1962_Asian_Games "1962 Asian Games"), the stadium had a seating capacity of 110,000. It has been reduced twice during renovations: first to 88,306 in 2006 for the [2007 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup") and then to 77,193 single seats as part of renovations for the [2018 Asian Games](/wiki/2018_Asian_Games "2018 Asian Games") and [Asian Para Games](/wiki/2018_Asian_Para_Games "2018 Asian Para Games"), where it hosted the ceremonies and athletics competitions. Due to the most recent renovation which saw all remaining bleachers replaced by single seats, it is the 28th largest association football stadium in the world and the 8th largest association football stadium in Asia.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Under Sukarno: construction and inauguration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Under Sukarno: construction and inauguration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Gelora_Bung_Karno_1962.jpg/250px-Gelora_Bung_Karno_1962.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gelora_Bung_Karno_1962.jpg)

The stadium under construction, April 1962

After the [Asian Games Federation](/wiki/Asian_Games_Federation "Asian Games Federation") declared Jakarta to host the [1962 Asian Games](/wiki/1962_Asian_Games "1962 Asian Games") in 1958, the minimum requirement that yet to be met by the [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") was the availability of a multi-sport complex. In response to this, President [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") issued Presidential Decree No. 113/1959 dated 11 May 1959 about the establishment of the Asian Games Council of Indonesia (DAGI) led by [Minister of Sports](https://id.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daftar_Menteri_Pemuda_dan_Olahraga_Indonesia "id:Daftar Menteri Pemuda dan Olahraga Indonesia") [Maladi](/wiki/Maladi "Maladi"). As an architect and civil engineering graduate, Sukarno proposed a location near M. H. Thamrin Boulevard and [Menteng](/wiki/Menteng "Menteng") ([Karet](/wiki/Karet_Tengsin,_Tanah_Abang "Karet Tengsin, Tanah Abang"), [Pejompongan](/wiki/Pejompongan "Pejompongan"), or [Dukuh Atas](/wiki/Dukuh_Atas_TOD "Dukuh Atas TOD")) for the future sports complex. then Sukarno accompanied [Friedrich Silaban](/wiki/Friedrich_Silaban "Friedrich Silaban"), a renowned architect to review the location of the proposed sports complex by helicopter. Silaban disagreed with the selection of Dukuh Atas because he argued the construction of a sports complex in the center the future downtown area will potentially create a massive traffic congestion. Sukarno agreed Silaban suggestion and instead assigned the Senayan area with an area of approximately 300 hectares.[[7]](#cite_note-11)

Construction began on 8 February 1960 and finished on 21 July 1962,[[8]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-12) in time to host the [following month's Asian Games](/wiki/1962_Asian_Games "1962 Asian Games"). It was built as part of Sukarno's construction sprees before the 1962 Asian Games and the centerpiece of the [Sports Complex](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Sports_Complex "Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex").[[9]](#cite_note-temugelang-13) Its construction was partially funded through a special loan from the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). The stadium's original capacity was 110,000 people. The stadium is well known for its gigantic ring-shaped [facade](/wiki/Facade "Facade") (a.k.a. "*temu gelang*"), as the world's first circular roof football stadium and also was designed to shade spectators from the sun, and increase the grandeur of the stadium.[[10]](#cite_note-14) The idea came from Sukarno himself and although Soviet architects didn't want to implement *temu gelang* at first due to its unusual design, but Sukarno insisted and he got his way in the end.[[9]](#cite_note-temugelang-13)
Although the stadium is popularly known as Gelora Bung Karno Stadium (*Stadion Gelora Bung Karno*) or *GBK* Stadium, its official name is Gelora Bung Karno Main Stadium (*Stadion Utama Gelora Bung Karno*), as there are other stadiums in the [Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Sports_Complex "Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex"), such as the [Sports Palace](/wiki/Istora_Gelora_Bung_Karno "Istora Gelora Bung Karno") and the [secondary stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Madya_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Madya Stadium"). It was known as Senajan ([EYD](/wiki/Enhanced_Indonesian_Spelling_System "Enhanced Indonesian Spelling System"): Senayan) Main Stadium from its opening through the 1962 Asiad until the complex's name was changed to Gelora Bung Karno by a Presidential Decree issued on 24 September 1962, twenty days after the games ended.

### Under Soeharto: *Gelora Senayan*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Under Soeharto: Gelora Senayan")]

During the [New Order](/wiki/New_Order_(Indonesia) "New Order (Indonesia)") era, the complex was renamed "Gelora Senayan Complex" and the stadium was renamed "Gelora Senayan Main Stadium" in 1969. The name changes was part of the "[de-Sukarnoization](/wiki/De-Sukarnoization "De-Sukarnoization")" policy by [military junta](/wiki/Military_junta "Military junta") government under [Suharto](/wiki/Suharto "Suharto").[[11]](#cite_note-15)

At the 1985 Perserikatan Final, Match [Persib Bandung](/wiki/Persib_Bandung "Persib Bandung") against [PSMS Medan](/wiki/PSMS_Medan "PSMS Medan") which was held at this stadium became an amateur match with the largest attendance of 150,000 spectators. The match was finally won by [PSMS Medan](/wiki/PSMS_Medan "PSMS Medan").[[5]](#cite_note-psms-8)

### *Reformasi*–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Reformasi–present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium%2C_Asia_Cup_2007.jpg/250px-Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium%2C_Asia_Cup_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium,_Asia_Cup_2007.jpg)

The stadium during the [2007 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/GBK_Main_Stadium_new_seats.jpg/250px-GBK_Main_Stadium_new_seats.jpg)](/wiki/File:GBK_Main_Stadium_new_seats.jpg)

The stadium with new seats, January 2018

Under reformation regime, the complex name was reverted by President [Abdurrahman Wahid](/wiki/Abdurrahman_Wahid "Abdurrahman Wahid") in a decree effective since 17 January 2001.[[12]](#cite_note-16) The stadium's capacity was then reduced further to 88,306 as a result of renovations for the [2007 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup").

The stadium served as the main venue of the [2018 Asian Games](/wiki/2018_Asian_Games "2018 Asian Games") and [Asian Para Games](/wiki/2018_Asian_Para_Games "2018 Asian Para Games"), hosting the ceremonies and athletics.[[13]](#cite_note-17) It underwent renovations in preparation for the events; to comply with [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") standards, all of the stadium's existing seating was replaced, including its remaining bleachers, making it an [all-seater](/wiki/All-seater_stadium "All-seater stadium") with a capacity of 77,193. The new seats are coloured in red, white, and grey—resembling a waving [flag of Indonesia](/wiki/Flag_of_Indonesia "Flag of Indonesia"). A new, brighter LED lighting system was also installed, with 620 fixtures, and an RGB lighting system was installed on the stadium's facade. Improvements were also made to the stadium's accessibility. The 2016–18 renovation of this stadium cost Rp769,69 billion (around US$59 million with 2016 exchange rate).[[14]](#cite_note-18)[[15]](#cite_note-19)[[16]](#cite_note-20)[[17]](#cite_note-21)[[18]](#cite_note-22)[[19]](#cite_note-23)

## Sporting events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Sporting events")]

GBK Stadium hosted the [2007 Asian Cup final](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup#Final "2007 AFC Asian Cup") between [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_national_football_team "Iraq national football team") and [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team"). Other competitions held there are several [AFF Cup](/wiki/AFF_Cup "AFF Cup") finals, [domestic cup](/wiki/Piala_Indonesia "Piala Indonesia") finals, [Liga 2](/wiki/Liga_2_(Indonesia) "Liga 2 (Indonesia)") Playoff and Finals, and [Liga Nusantara](/wiki/Liga_3_(Indonesia) "Liga 3 (Indonesia)") Playoff and Finals.

## Other uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Other uses")]

Note:[[e]](#cite_note-24)

* The Grand [Catholic mass](/wiki/Catholic_mass "Catholic mass") led by [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI"), on 3 December 1970; [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II"), on 9 October 1989 and [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") on 5 September 2024.[[20]](#cite_note-25)[[21]](#cite_note-26)[[22]](#cite_note-27)
* The 100th anniversary of [Indonesian National Awakening](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Awakening "Indonesian National Awakening") day, 20 May 2008[[23]](#cite_note-28)
* The political rally for both parliamentary and also presidential elections in 2004, 2009, 2014, 2019, and 2024. The 2019 final day campaign for both presidential candidates was held in this stadium. The final campaign was held on 7 and 13 April 2019 respectively. Each final campaign was attended by more than 77,000 supporters, arguably the most attended a one-day campaign rally in the history of the Indonesian presidential campaign.[[24]](#cite_note-29)
* Christmas event jointly organized by the [Indonesian Bethel Church](/wiki/Indonesian_Bethel_Church "Indonesian Bethel Church") for the whole district (2006–2011, 2013–)
* [Indonesia Tiberias Church](https://id.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gereja_Tiberias_Indonesia "id:Gereja Tiberias Indonesia") Christmas Services (2000–2015, 2018–)[[25]](#cite_note-30)
* [HKBP](/wiki/HKBP "HKBP") Jubileum (147th in 2007 and 150th in 2011)[[26]](#cite_note-31)
* The 85th anniversary of [Nahdlatul Ulama](/wiki/Nahdlatul_Ulama "Nahdlatul Ulama") (2011)[[27]](#cite_note-32)
* Caliphate Conference of [Hizb ut-Tahrir](/wiki/Hizb_ut-Tahrir "Hizb ut-Tahrir") Indonesia, 6 June 2013[[28]](#cite_note-33)
* Admission exams for thousands [Indonesian Ministry of Health](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_(Indonesia) "Ministry of Health (Indonesia)") civil servants applicants on 3 November 2013[[29]](#cite_note-34)
* One of the venues in Jakarta used for [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") vaccination serving 60,000 doses of vaccines, 11 July 2021.[[30]](#cite_note-35)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/MRT_Jakarta_trainset_in_Istora_Mandiri_MRT_station.jpg/250px-MRT_Jakarta_trainset_in_Istora_Mandiri_MRT_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:MRT_Jakarta_trainset_in_Istora_Mandiri_MRT_station.jpg)

[Istora Mandiri MRT station](/wiki/Istora_Mandiri_MRT_station "Istora Mandiri MRT station"), the nearest [Jakarta MRT](/wiki/MRT_Jakarta "MRT Jakarta") station to the stadium complex.

[KRL Commuterline](/wiki/KRL_Commuterline "KRL Commuterline") provides transport service through [Palmerah railway station](/wiki/Palmerah_railway_station "Palmerah railway station") within walking distance from the compound, while [Jakarta MRT](/wiki/MRT_Jakarta "MRT Jakarta") provides service through [Istora Mandiri station](/wiki/Istora_Mandiri_MRT_station "Istora Mandiri MRT station"). Two corridors of [Transjakarta](/wiki/Transjakarta "Transjakarta") BRT also serve this area. An extension of the [Jabodebek LRT](/wiki/Jabodebek_LRT "Jabodebek LRT") is also planned to serve the western perimeter of the compound.
